svg.text: POOR MARY SITS A-WEEPING
Instructions
What You Need
Nothing special — just the game board on page 1.
How to Play
One player sits in the center of a circle, head bowed, pretending to weep.
The circle of players walks around them singing, asking why Mary weeps. Mary
dramatically announces her reason (lost love, loneliness — hamming it up is
encouraged). The group sings encouragement — choose someone to love, be
happy! Mary stands, picks a partner from the ring, and the two act out the
wedding verse together while the group sings. The chosen partner becomes the
new weeping Mary, and the game repeats.
Did You Know?
Poor Mary Sits A-Weeping was recorded by Gomme in 20 variants across Britain,
making it one of the most geographically widespread British singing games.
Scholars believe it preserves ancient mourning rituals transformed into play.
Original Victorian Description
One child sits weeping in the center of a ring. The ring sings and asks why
Mary weeps. She names her reason — sweetheart lost, father dead. The ring
comforts her, telling her to choose a lover and marry. Variants include going
to church, placing a ring on, and the couple kneeling together. The song:
'Poor Mary sits a-weeping / On a bright summer's day.'
Source: The Traditional Games of England, Scotland, and Ireland, Vol. II (1898)
Heritage Parlor · Page 2 of 2
